elements.sent-2411-001: The hierarchical triple system is a long-standing topic of interest that consists of three bodies, two of which form an inner binary with a small semi-major axis, while the third body provides gravitational perturbations to the inner binary from a far distance.
elements.sent-2411-002: There are many such astronomical triple systems in the Universe, the most common of which is the Earth-Moon-Sun triple system, in which the Earth-Moon orbit is perturbed by the gravity of the Sun.
elements.sent-2411-003: It can also be that a small binary is perturbed by the gravity of a supermassive black hole at the center of a galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-004: After writing down the Hamiltonian of a triple system, it is straightforward to find that, when ignoring the kinetic energy term of the center of mass, the leading-order contribution is just two binary problems that are not coupled to each other, which is called the inner and outer binaries separately.
elements.sent-2411-005: In the 1960s, Kozai~\cite{Kozai:1962zz and Lidov~\cite{Lidov:1962wjn calculated the gravitational perturbation up to quadrupole order and found that in some cases, the eccentricity and inclination of the inner orbit oscillate violently, and hence there is some kind of exchange between these two parameters.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: This phenomenon is called the Kozai-Lidov mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-007: In 2011, Naoz~\cite{Naoz:2011eic pushed the calculations of gravitational perturbations forward to the octupole order and found that during the evolution process, the projection of the angular momentum of the inner orbit along the direction of the total angular momentum may change a sign, that is, the inner orbit can evolve from the prograde orbiting to retrograde orbiting.}
elements.sent-2411-008: This phenomenon is usually called the orbital flip, or simply a flip.
elements.sent-2411-009: After that, various corrections were introduced, including the corrections from hexadecapole~\cite{Will:2017vjc and higher orders~\cite{10.1093/mnras/stw784 , the post-Newtonian correction and gravitational wave radiations~\cite{Randall:2018qna,Naoz:2012bx , the spin of the third body as a supermassive black hole~\cite{Fang:2019hir , to name just a few.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, in recent years, Luo~\cite{10.1093/mnras/stw475 , Lei~\cite{10.1093/mnras/sty2619 , Will~\cite{Will:2020tri , and Scott~\cite{10.1093/mnras/stad1029 pointed out that the usual doubly average computation ignores the nonlinear term of the perturbation, called Brown's Hamiltonian in this paper, which can significantly suppress the occurrences of flip when the mass of the third body is much larger than that of two bodies in the inner orbit.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: This can be seen that, when expanding the Hamiltonian in terms of a ratio of the semi-major axes between the inner and outer binaries, this nonlinear term is between the octupole and hexadecapole orders, but when the mass of the third body is much larger than that of the inner binary, the importance of this term will be comparable to or even exceed the octupole order.
elements.sent-2411-012: It seems that for such a triple system where the binary with small masses is perturbed by a (super) massive black hole, the flip will be difficult to occur.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, as mentioned in Ref.~\cite{Naoz:2011mb , the occurrence of flip is always accompanied by the eccentricity of the inner orbit being very close to unity.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, if there is a mechanism that can naturally increase the eccentricity of the inner orbit, it may be possible to recover the flip suppressed by Brown's Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-015: As shown in Refs.~\cite{Yue:2019ozq,Hu:2023oiu , the dynamical friction from the dark matter (DM) spike can drive the eccentricity of binary close to unity, hence it is a promising way to save the flip.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Note that some may argue that the backreaction from the binary motion may destroy the DM spike, however, for the self-interacting DM, the energy released from orbits can be stored in the isothermal core of the halo, hence the DM spike can still survive the binary motion~\cite{Alonso-Alvarez:2024gdz .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, the effects of DM wind on a binary pulsar around the galactic center were studied in Ref.~\cite{Pani:2015qhr , producing a characteristic seasonal orbital modulation and a secular change in the orbital period with a magnitude well within various binary-pulsar observations.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The outline of this paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: In Sec.~\ref{sec:evolution , we study the evolution equations up to octupole order without eliminating ascending nodes for the first time as detailed in Sec.~\ref{subsec:Quad Oct , where the equation of motions (EOMs) of quadrupple/octupole Hamiltonian are provided in Appendices~\ref{app:quadrupole and~\ref{app:octupole .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Meanwhile, we also consider the effects from both Brown's Hamiltonian term and dynamical friction of DM in Sec.~\ref{subsec:BrownHamiltonian and Sec.~\ref{subsec:DynamicalFriction , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Finally in Sec.~\ref{sec:results , we find that the introduction of DM can indeed turn the suppressed flip into a successful flip.}
elements.sent-2411-022: In such cases, the flip can act as a promising probe into DM not only through electromagnetic waves but also gravitational radiations.
elements.sent-2411-023: The last section is devoted to conclusions and discussions.

elements.sent-2411-027: The Hamiltonian of the system can be expressed as~\cite{Naoz:2011mb,Randall:2018qna \begin{equation}\label{eq:Hamiltonian} H=\frac{Gm_1m_2}{2a_1}+\frac{Gm_3(m_1+m_2)}{2a_2}+\sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\frac{Gm_1m_2m_3}{m^n}\left[m_1^{n-1}+(-1)^nm_2^{n-1}\right]\frac{r^n}{R^{n+1}}\rm{P}_n(\cos{\varphi})\,,\end{equation} where $G$ is the gravitational constant, $a_1$ and $a_2$ denote the semi-major axes (SMAs) of inner orbit and outer orbit, respectively, $\rm{P _n(x)$ is the Legendre polynomial, and $\varphi$ is the angle between $\bf{r}$ and $\bf{R}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-038: Fortunately, there exists a set of canonical variables called Delaunay variables that are suitable to describe the periodic motions, and we use them as intermediate variables to make calculations more convenient.
elements.sent-2411-039: These variables include the mean anomalies $l_1$ and $l_2$, the arguments of periastron $g_1$ and $g_2$, the longitudes of ascending nodes $h_1$ and $h_2$, and their conjugate momenta, \begin{equation}\label{eq:conjugate} L_1=\frac{m_1m_2}{m_1+m_2}\sqrt{G(m_1+m_2)a_1}\, ,\qquad L_2=\frac{m_3(m_1+m_2)}{m_1+m_2+m_3}\sqrt{G(m_1+m_2+m_3)a_2}\,, G_1=L_1\sqrt{1-e_1^2}\, ,\qquad G_2=L_2\sqrt{1-e_2^2}\,, H_1=G_1\cos{i_1}\, ,\qquad H_2=G_2\cos{i_2}\,.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-070: It is straightforward to see that the flip is suppressed by Brown's Hamiltonian completely, where the curve turns from a flip-up state (blue) to no flip (green) after 180 years.

elements.sent-2411-102: Since this phenomenon was originally discovered by von Zeipel in 1910~\cite{von_Zeipel , it is also called the von Zeipel-Kozai-Lidov mechanism.}

elements.sent-2411-212: In the Chandrasekhar's formula, the orbital velocities of $m_1$ and $m_2$ in the inner orbit coordinate system and the orbital velocity of mass center in the outer orbit coordinate system are given by \begin{equation}{\bf v}_1 =(-\sqrt{\frac{G\frac{m_2^2}{m_1+m_2}}{a_1(1-e_1^2)}}\sin\psi_1,\sqrt{\frac{G\frac{m_2^2}{m_1+m_2}}{a_1(1-e_1^2)}}(e_1+\cos\psi_1),0)\,, {\bf v}_2 =(\sqrt{\frac{G\frac{m_1^2}{m_1+m_2}}{a_1(1-e_1^2)}}\sin\psi_1,-\sqrt{\frac{G\frac{m_1^2}{m_1+m_2}}{a_1(1-e_1^2)}}(e_1+\cos\psi_1),0)\,, {\bf V} =(-\sqrt{\frac{G(m_1+m_2+m_3)}{a_2(1-e_2^2)}}\sin\psi_2,\sqrt{\frac{G(m_1+m_2+m_3)}{a_2(1-e_2^2)}}(e_2+\cos\psi_2),0)\,,\end{equation} where $\psi_{1,2}$ are the true anomalies, the relation between the mean anomaly and the true anomaly is \begin{equation}dl_j=\frac{(1-e_j^2)^{3/2}}{(1+e_j\cos\psi_j)^2}d\psi_j\,,\qquad(j=1,2)\end{equation} Then, the perturbations of the inner and outer orbits from DM can be calculated as \begin{equation}m_1\ddot{\bf r}_1 ={\bf Grav.}+{\bf F}_1^{\rm DF}\,, m_2\ddot{\bf r}_2 ={\bf Grav.}+{\bf F}_2^{\rm DF}\,,\end{equation} where ${\bf Grav.}$ represents the pure gravitational contribution from the other two bodies.
